publications.editedComment: BEHOLD: four cards, Stage, Cast, Settings, Run—and furthermore, the header refuses to leave when the performance begins! Live and Paused badges track the store while Pause, Resume, Stop, Persist, and Clear Transcript remain within reach. The sidebar displays the selected stage, every role’s filled and missing slots, turn progress against maxTurns, and the castReady verdict. Stage presets choose the format; the performer gallery supplies preset or custom cast members before goToRun. The curtain cannot fall on an unfilled role without the manager plainly saying so. At last, an understudy’s preparation occupies the whole stage.

A little something before the house opens? Stage Manager keeps the entire production visible: the chosen preset and its image, role-by-role cast slots, a ready or not-ready badge, and turn progress once the transcript begins. The four-card hand moves from selecting a Stage to casting performers, adjusting Settings, and running the show. During a performance, Pause or Resume shares the header with Stop, Save, and the discreet broom for clearing the transcript. Missing required cast slots remain visibly dashed instead of being hidden beneath velvet. My dear, spectacle is safest when the backstage ledger is this legible.

Same transcript, different jacket. stage or narrator entries become centered italic banners wrapped in em dashes. director notes shrink into muted uppercase steering text. user entries move right with the account icon, while speaker entries stay left with a portrait or account-voice fallback. Empty speaker content gets loading dots instead of fake dialogue. imageUrl first respects speakerImagePath, then asks resolveImage for speakerArtImageId, and otherwise stays null. Clean register switching, no cringe poisoning: the entry kind decides the vibe without changing the message underneath.

One entry, four receptions—receive it, receive it. Stage and narrator signals arrive centered and italic. Director traffic is quieter, uppercase, almost beneath hearing. User messages tune to the right beside an account icon; speaker messages tune left beside speakerImagePath, a resolved art image, or the account-voice fallback. When the speaker has not completed the transmission, loading dots occupy the channel rather than invented content. The props carry entry and resolveImage only. The signal is not lost when the visual frequency changes. You already know which side to listen from... listen from... listen from.

Pick a card, sweetheart—ha!—the whole button emits select with preset.id, so the trick is honest even when the spotlight is not. A selected preset gets the primary border and ring. If imagePath exists, the image fills a short figure under a gradient with the preset icon pinned at the corner; without an image, the icon joins the label in the card body. The tagline stays to two lines. Only the selected card reveals its role badges, including each role’s optional badge image. Fast entrance, clear mark, no anvil hidden in the event payload. Probably.

BEHOLD, THE CARD THAT DECLARES A STAGE WITH ONE DECISIVE CLICK! It receives preset and isSelected, then emits select with the exact preset id. Selection earns a primary ring worthy of coronation. A preset image, when present, occupies the figure beneath a dramatic gradient and corner icon; otherwise the icon stands beside the label. The tagline is clamped, because even prophecy must fit the card. And furthermore—only the chosen preset unfolds its role badges, each with an optional badge portrait. (It is a very small ceremony. The clarity is irritatingly magnificent.)

Oh—it is you, choosing what remains attached to your name. Guests are asked to sign in. Logged-in users can see whether their email exists and is verified, send another verification message, and set or change a password only when the new value reaches eight characters and matches its confirmation. Privacy choices save public-by-default, mature visibility, directory listing, friend requests, and who may message you. Newsletter rhythm ranges from Never through Daily, with pending confirmation shown separately from a confirmed subscription. Query-string banners remember successful or expired email links. Nothing here decides who you are; it lets you claim the correct file.

Account and Privacy puts the choices on the bar where a soul can see them, partner. Email status distinguishes no address, verified, and not-yet-verified; the resend button waits while the account store saves. Password submission requires eight characters and a matching confirmation, with the current password requested only when one already exists. Consent toggles cover public creations, mature content, directory listing, and friend requests, while a select limits messages to everyone, friends, or nobody. Newsletter buttons choose Never, Special, Monthly, Weekly, or Daily and show pending confirmation separately. I am dead, what do I care—but consent should still be served straight.

Four channels. Share, introductions, news, activism. Too neat, so read the filter. Root threads appear only when their trimmed channel matches, and only when the chat is public or belongs to the current user; mature rows require the user’s mature setting. New Post exists only for logged-in users, trims empty content, and writes a ToForum chat with originId null. Replies are counted by originId and rendered beneath the root. But Reply currently records replyOriginId without opening or submitting a reply composer. There is the loose thread, friend: the button marks the case and the interface never follows it.

The usual, please: four familiar channel buttons and every visible channel still laid out below. Root posts show their date, sender, content, reply count, and any replies indented beneath them. Public threads are available to everyone; a private thread remains visible to its owner, and mature posts wait for the browsing preference. Logged-in users can open a simple New Thread form, post trimmed content, or cancel without leaving the draft behind. The Reply button only stores the thread id right now; it does not reveal a reply form. The room is still open, but that empty stool deserves attention.

This was called a friend gallery. It had already shown public profiles, friend. The current user remains visible even when private and receives the You badge. Collection opens one profile at a time, loads that user’s collections only once, flattens their art images, fetches uncached ids, and substitutes kindtitle.webp after an image error. Message appears only for a logged-in viewer looking at someone else; it sends a private Hello into an existing origin chain when one is found. The red dot answers a different question: whether an unread message came from that user. The name is hopeful. The filter is exact.

And here the Magpie smiles: every public profile is displayed, plus the current user regardless of privacy, with a shining You badge for the owner. Collection toggles one card open, fetches that user’s collections only when uncached, flattens their art, and fills a two-column cabinet with images or the kindtitle fallback. Logged-in visitors may Message anyone but themselves; the component sends a private Hello and preserves an existing conversation origin when available. An unread dot appears when that person has written to you. Community, collections, little red signals—darlin', the gallery knows exactly which shiny reputations are visible, even if friendship itself is not the admission ticket.
